# Coding Standards

Use this skill when implementing, refactoring, or reviewing project code.

## Principles

- Prefer clear names, small focused functions, and the simplest solution that meets the stated requirement.
- Avoid speculative abstractions; add complexity only when the requirement or evidence justifies it.
- Default to immutable values and explicit data flow. Isolate intentional mutation with a rationale.
- Keep public interfaces typed and validate untrusted input at boundaries.
- Make asynchronous work explicit, handle failure paths, and avoid hidden side effects.
- Write focused tests for observable behaviour, including invalid inputs and boundary conditions.

## Review checklist

- [ ] Names describe intent and units where relevant.
- [ ] The change does not duplicate existing behaviour or introduce unnecessary coupling.
- [ ] Public interfaces and errors are documented or self-evident through types.
- [ ] Tests demonstrate the acceptance criteria and important failure cases.
- [ ] Comments explain non-obvious rationale rather than restating code.

## Provenance

Curated adaptation of ECC `skills/coding-standards/SKILL.md` at commit `ed387446052dfbc6b52de149406b70efa65edc59`, MIT License. Framework-specific, package-install, and external-service examples were intentionally excluded.
